segments.s464-g33: ببخشید is an archaic/polished past-form meaning 'he gave' or 'he bestowed' (here active, 3rd person). بر introduces the beneficiary/recipient ('upon/to'), لشکرش is the noun 'his army' with the possessive -ش, and خواسته is a noun 'the wished/desired (thing)' — together: 'he bestowed upon his army what he desired.' Word order places the verb before the prepositional indirect object and the noun denoting what was given.
segments.s464-g34: به is the preposition 'with/by/to' marking accompaniment or instrument, نیرو here means a military 'force/troops', سپاهی functions as an attributive noun/adjective referring to 'soldiers/military', شد is the past 'became' and آراسته is a past participle 'adorned/arrayed' used predicatively. The clause means 'it was arrayed/adorned with military force' (result state expressed by شد + adjectival آراسته).
segments.s464-g35: ببود is an archaic past copular/existential 'was/were' (classical usage). اندران (= در آن) is a fused locative 'in that/therein', بوم و بر is the idiomatic pair 'land and realm/soil and region', and چار ماه gives the duration 'four months' — overall: 'they were in that land and region for four months.' The order is slightly inverted (predicate before locative expression) as is common in epic style.
segments.s464-g36: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when', آسوده is an adjective 'at ease/relieved', and شد is the past 'became' (copula). شهریار و سپاه are the coordinated subjects 'the king and the army' that follow the verb here (verb–subject order is common in poetry). The clause means 'when the king and the army became at ease.'
segments.s464-g37: جهاندار is an epithet 'world-ruler/sovereign' and is followed by دارا (here a title/name meaning 'possessor' used as the king's name); به + جهرم is the prepositional phrase 'to Jahrom', and رسید is the simple past 'reached/arrived'. The sequence is a canonical S(Title/Name) + prepositional destination + past verb.
segments.s464-g38: که introduces a relative/time clause 'where/that', آنجا is the locative adverb 'there'. بدی here functions as an archaic past form meaning 'gave' (classical usage of a giving-verb form) rather than the noun 'evil' listed in the lemma, so that listed sense does not match the usage (marked below). گنجها را is the definite object 'the treasures' marked by را, and کلید is 'the key' — poetically the object phrase follows the verb, so the clause reads 'where he gave there the key of the treasures.'
segments.s464-g39: همه means 'all/everyone'. مهتران (in classical usage) means 'the chiefs/elders/leaders' (the supplied lemma senses do not reflect this exact sense, so the sense index is marked accordingly). پیش here is an adverb meaning 'before/in the presence (of someone)' or 'forward/near', باز is the adverb 'again/away/back' used with آمدند to give the sense 'came forward/returned', and آمدند is the past plural 'they came'. The clause describes all the chiefs advancing/presenting themselves.
segments.s464-g40: پر ... از is the fixed pattern 'full of' (پر = 'full', از = 'of/from'), درد is 'pain' here best read as emotional sorrow/affliction, and گرم و گداز are figurative descriptors 'hot/ardent' and 'melting/anguish' (a poetic pair describing intense inner suffering). آمدند repeats the past plural 'they came' — the whole phrase: 'they came, full of pain and burning anguish.'
segments.s464-g41: خروشان is a poetic adjectival/participial modifier placed before the noun (instead of the more common post-nominal position) meaning ‘howling/roaring’ and describes پسر. چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning ‘when’. پدر را is the definite object (را marks the object/topic), and ندید is the past simple verb ‘did (not) see’. The clause literally reads ‘the howling son, when he did not see the father.’
segments.s464-g42: This clause mirrors the previous one. پدر is the subject, همچنین is an adverb meaning ‘likewise/similarly’, and چون again introduces the temporal clause ‘when’. پسر را is the object (marked by را) and ندید is the past verb ‘did not see’. The order is subject + adverb + temporal subordinate clause + object + verb.
segments.s464-g43: همه شهر ایران is a noun phrase meaning ‘the whole/entire city of Iran’ (همه as ‘whole/entire’ modifies شهر ایران). پر از ناله بود is the predicate: پر از (+ noun) ‘full of’ + ناله ‘lament’ and بود is the past copula ‘was’. Word order is subject phrase → predicate → copula.
segments.s464-g44: به چشم اندرون is a locative phrase ‘in/within the inner eye(s)’ (به + noun + اندرون). آب is the noun subject (‘water’, figuratively tears), and چون introduces a simile ‘like/as’; ژاله is the comparison term ‘dew’ and بود is the past copula ‘was’. The clause reads ‘in the inner eye the water was like dew.’
segments.s464-g45: ز جهرم is a prepositional phrase ‘from Jahrom’ (ز is the poetic form of از). بیامد is the past tense verb ‘came’. به شهر صطخر is the destination phrase ‘to the city of Istakhr’ (به + شهر + place name). The clause is a simple past motion clause: from X came to Y.
segments.s464-g46: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause qualifying the city. آزادگان را is marked with را (topic/object marker) meaning ‘to/for the free/nobles’, بران here functions adverbially ‘thereon/for them’ (i.e. ‘to them’), بود is the copula ‘was’, and فخر is the predicate noun ‘pride’. Together: ‘which was pride to the nobles.’
segments.s464-g47: فرستاده‌ای is an indefinite noun phrase ‘a messenger’ (literal participle/agent of ‘to send’ used as a person). رفت is the past tense ‘went’. بر هر سوی is an adverbial phrase formed with بر + هر + سوی meaning ‘toward every direction/into every quarter’. The clause means ‘a messenger went to every direction.’
segments.s464-g48: به هر نامداری و هر پهلوی is a prepositional list of recipients/targets: به ‘to’ + هر نامداری ‘each notable/person of renown’ and هر پهلوی ‘each side/flank’ (هر repeated for emphasis). The phrase specifies to whom/which quarters the messengers were sent.
segments.s464-g65: چنین هم نماند بیاید کنون is a small clause made of an adverbial phrase + verb cluster. چنین هم نماند = 'such (a state) did not remain either' — نماند is the past/perfect form with negative prefix ن- on ماندن, meaning 'did not remain/last.' بیاید (present/subjunctive/impersonal classical form of آمدن) follows as an impersonal or resumptive verb meaning 'it comes / it will come to pass,' and کنون is a temporal adverb 'now' that scopes the whole clause. The subject is omitted (impersonal/abstract).
segments.s464-g66: همه پارس گردد چو دریای خون is a prediction/comparative clause. همه پارس = 'the whole of Pars / all of Persia' (همه qualifies پارس). گردد is the classical 3rd-person singular optative/subjunctive of گشتن/گردیدن meaning 'will become / shall be turned into.' چو introduces a simile 'like/as,' and دریای خون = 'a sea of blood' (دریای is the construct form of دریا). The construction states: 'the whole of Pars will become as a sea of blood.'
segments.s464-g67: زن و کودک و مرد گردند اسیر lists subjects then the verb and predicate: the nouns 'women, children, and men' come first, گردند is the classical 3rd-person plural of گشتن/گردیدن meaning 'they become' (resultative/passive sense), and اسیر is a predicative noun 'captives.' Word order is topical list → verb → predicate, so: 'women, children, and men became captives.'
segments.s464-g68: نماند برین بوم برنا و پیر uses an inversive order: نماند 'there remains not / no longer remains' (negative perfect/past sense of ماندن) followed by the locative phrase برین بوم 'on this land' (بر + این written as برین) and the coordinated nouns برنا و پیر 'the young and the old.' Literally: 'On this land the young and the old do not remain' → 'neither young nor old remains on this land.'
segments.s464-g69: مرا‌گر شوید اندرین یارمند is a conditional protasis with an archaic particle-ordering: مراگر (مرا + گر) = 'if to me' / 'if (you are) for me'; شوید is 2nd-person plural subjunctive/present of شدن 'you become' (classical polite/plural form), and اندرین یارمند = 'in this (matter) allies/helpers.' Together the clause means 'if you become helpers/allies to me in this.' The construction uses classical conditional گر + verb.
segments.s464-g70: بگردانم این رنج و درد و گزند has a first-person singular verb بگردانم (present/subjunctive/formal-future sense of گرداندن used here to mean 'I will turn/avert/drive away'), followed by the direct object list این رنج و درد و گزند 'this suffering and pain and harm.' The verb is transitive and promises action contingent on the protasis above: 'I will avert this suffering, pain, and harm.'
segments.s464-g71: شکار بزرگان بدند این گروه places the predicate (شکار بزرگان 'prey/quarry of the great ones') before the verb بدند (classical 3rd-person plural of بودن/شدن 'they were / they became'), with the topic/subject این گروه 'this group' at the end — an archaic topical order. The meaning is 'this group became the prey of the great ones' (i.e., were captured or plundered by the great ones).
segments.s464-g72: همه گشته از شهر ایران ستوه has همه 'all/everyone' followed by گشته (perfect/participial form of شدن 'have become / have gone') and the locative/origination phrase از شهر ایران 'from the city of Iran,' ending with the predicate adjective ستوه 'weary / sick of.' The clause reads: 'All have become (i.e., are) weary of the city of Iran' or more idiomatically 'all are exhausted/afflicted from the city of Iran.'
segments.s464-g89: ببندیم is the 1st-person-plural subjunctive/optative of بستن (here 'to bind/fasten'), functioning like an exhortation 'let us bind'. The verb is placed before its object (poetic order). دامن یک اندر دگر is a folded noun phrase: دامن 'hem/skirt' followed by the idiomatic spatial phrase یک اندر دگر 'one into the other/one to another', i.e. 'bind our hems together.'
segments.s464-g90: The pair اگر ... اگر repeats the conditional particle for emphasis ('if ... if'). یابیم is the 1st-person-plural subjunctive/present form of یافتن and here signals the conditional result 'if we find' (the grammatical force of یابیم is subjunctive). خاک and the fixed phrase بوم و بر name the things sought: خاک 'soil/earth' and بوم و بر 'home and land' (broadly: homeland/territory). The verb یابیم from this clause is linked back to the exhortation 'ببندیم'.
segments.s464-g91: داد is the simple past 3rd-person singular 'he gave'. The phrase places the items given (سلیح 'weapons' and درم 'dirhams') before the verb, while لشکرش را ('his army' + object marker را) names the recipient; in poetic/older Persian the recipient can follow the verb and still be marked by را. را here is the definite/object marker marking the recipient/beneficiary.
segments.s464-g92: همان is an intensifier 'that very' / 'the same' pointing back to another recipient; نامداران کشورش is a noun phrase 'the famed/renowned ones of his country' (نامداران as plural substantive/adjective with کشورش 'his country'). The final را again marks this group as an additional definite recipient; the verb داد from the previous segment is understood to govern this phrase as well.
segments.s464-g73: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. ما شکاریم is a copular clause where شکار (here a noun) is predicate and -یم is the 1st-person plural copula/ending: literally 'we are prey/quarry.' The second clause (ایشان پلنگ) omits the copula (understood 'are') — a common Persian ellipsis — so it reads 'and they (are) leopards.'
segments.s464-g74: به هر کارزاری is a locative/occasional phrase meaning 'in/at every campaign/engagement' (به + هر + کارزاری). گریزَان is a present participle/adjectival participle 'fleeing/avoiding' and ز جنگ is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from battle/war' (ز = from). The whole string qualifies the preceding clause: 'they are leopards — (yet) in every engagement (they are) fleeing from battle.'
segments.s464-g75: اگر introduces a conditional. پشت یکسر 'پشت' (back/rear) with یکسر 'entirely/wholly' forms the object/phrase 'their whole rear'; به پشت آورید uses آوردن ('to bring/turn') in the present/subjunctive/optative 2nd-person plural form (آورید) after the condition — here meaning 'if you turn/bring (them) onto their backs' or 'if you utterly rout them.' Note the repetition of پشت emphasizes the idea of turning/reversing onto the back.
segments.s464-g76: بر و بوم is a fixed phrase meaning 'towns and homeland / country' (literally 'roofs and dwellings'), ایشان 'their/them', and به مشت آورید again uses آوردن with مشت 'fist' to mean 'bring into your fist' — idiomatically 'subdue/bring under your control.' The verb آورید repeats the conditional result: 'and (so) bring their lands into your fist.'
segments.s464-g77: کسی 'anyone/someone' is the indefinite subject; کاندرین is an archaic fused locative/adverbial meaning 'in this (circumstance)/herein' (kān dar in). جنگ 'battle' is the domain, and سستی کند is a verbal phrase: سستی 'weakness' + کند (3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of کردن) meaning 'shows/does weakness' — i.e., 'is lax or falters in the fight.'
segments.s464-g78: بکوشد is a 3rd-person singular optative/subjunctive ('let him strive' or 'he should strive'); که تا is a purposive/meshing conjunctional pair ('that/in order that') introducing the purpose clause; جان‌پرستی is a nominal verbal compound 'self‑preservation' (lit. 'life‑preserving/self‑preservation'); کند is the light verb 'do' (here marking the action: 'practice self‑preservation'). The whole yields: 'let him strive so that he may preserve his life.'
segments.s464-g79: مدارید is an imperative/ hortative plural meaning 'refrain' / 'do not expect' (here advising restraint). ازین پس 'from this after' = 'henceforth / from now on' (از + این (written as ین here) + پس). به گیتی امید places the object 'hope' toward the world: امید 'hope' with the prepositional phrase به گیتی 'in/to the world' — overall: 'henceforth do not place hope in the world.'
segments.s464-g80: که introduces a declarative subordinate clause 'that/for'; شد is the past of شدن 'became.' روم is the subject 'Rome/the Romans' (here meaning the Roman/Byzantine polity or Romans), ضحاک is the predicative name 'Zahhāk' (i.e., turned into Zahhāk), and و ما جمشید ellipses the verb (شد) and reads 'and we (became) Jamshīd.' The clause states a historical/metaphorical reversal: 'Rome became Zahhāk and we (became) Jamshīd.'
segments.s464-g81: همی (an emphatic/adverbial particle) here marks continuous or ongoing action ('kept' / 'still'). گفت is the verb 'said' in past form but used with همی to convey an ongoing utterance; گریان is a present-participial adjective 'weeping' describing the manner of speaking. دل پر ز درد is a small noun phrase inserted after the verb: دل (heart) + پر (full (of)) + ز (poetic preposition 'of/from') + درد (pain), giving 'heart full of pain' and linking as the speaker’s internal state.
segments.s464-g82: This noun phrase lists physical features in parallel: دو (two) modifies رخساره (face/cheek), زرد is a post-nominal adjective 'pale/yellow'; و connects the two coordinated phrases; دو لب = 'two lips' and لاژورد (a poetic color/stone name) describes their hue. Word order is noun–adjective (رخساره زرد) and the adjectives are tersely placed after the nouns, typical in classical Persian.
segments.s464-g83: بزرگان داننده is a noun phrase in which بزرگان ('the great/important ones') is modified by داننده ('knowing, learned'), giving 'the learned elders'. برخاستند is a simple past/plural verb 'they rose/stood up' agreeing with the plural subject — a straightforward subject–predicate structure.
segments.s464-g84: همه is subject or emphatic 'all/everyone'; پاسخش is 'his answer' (پاسخ + possessive -ش), followed by the object marker را which flags it as definite/explicit object; بیاراستند is a past plural verb 'they arranged/composed/put in order' (here 'prepared/phrased' the answer). The word order is Subject – Object – Verb with را marking the object.
segments.s464-g85: خروشی is a verbal noun meaning 'a cry/outcry' or 'commotion' and برآمد is the past verb 'rose/issued forth'; ز ایران is the source phrase 'from Iran' using the poetic preposition ز; به زار is an adverbial phrase where به + زار (زار here 'lament/wail') yields 'in lament' or 'lamentingly', so the whole clause means 'a lamenting cry arose from Iran.'
segments.s464-g86: که introduces the content clause (that/that which): گیتی 'the world' is the subject; نخواهیم is 1st‑person plural present/optative/negated verb 'we will not want / we will not allow' (classical usage often functions hortatively/decisively); بی‌شهریار is a compound adjective 'without a king / kingless'. The clause reads as a firm statement/refusal that the world shall be left kingless.
segments.s464-g87: همه روی یکسر is an adverbial/subject phrase: همه 'all/everyone' + روی 'faces' + یکسر 'entirely/at once', expressing collective, wholehearted action. به جنگ آوریم is a first‑person plural subjunctive/hortative construction often translated 'let us bring to war' or 'let us take up war'; آوریم (we bring) here carries the hortatory sense 'let us do X' rather than a simple descriptive present.
segments.s464-g88: جهان is 'the world'; بر here functions as a preposition 'upon/against' introducing the target of the action; بداندیش 'ill‑minded' qualifies those targeted; تنگ 'narrow' is used predicatively with آوریم (we will make/bring) so the literal structure is 'we will bring/make the world narrow upon the ill‑minded' — i.e., we will press/oppose the wicked. Word order places the object (جهان) first for emphasis, then the prepositional target and the verb.
segments.s464-g1: "چو" is a temporal conjunction meaning "when" introducing a subordinate clause; the clause's subject is دارا (Dāra) and the prepositional phrase ز پیش سکندر "from Alexander's presence/in front of Alexander" (ز + پیش + name) marks source/location; برفت is an archaic past form (3rd sg.) meaning "he went/left" and comes at the end as the clause's verb.
segments.s464-g2: به هر سو is an adverbial phrase "toward every side/in every direction" (به + هر + سو). سواران is the direct object "horsemen/riders" placed before the verb, and فرستاد is the past transitive verb "he sent/dispatched." تفت here functions as a following noun (likely a place-name or destination) but its lexicon senses in the provided list do not match this use.
segments.s464-g3: از ایران is a source phrase "from Iran." سران و مهان را is the coordinated definite object (را marking the object) meaning "the chiefs and the guests/nobles." بخواند here is a classical/persuasive past meaning "he summoned/called (them)."
segments.s464-g4: درم داد is a short clause "he gave dirhams" (درم = coin, داد = gave). و links the clauses. روزی here refers to "daily provision/ration" (the provided senses don't list this sense), دهان را is the object marked by را (literally "the mouths," idiomatically meaning "for the people"), and بخواند is used in the sense "to assign/appoint/provide for" (classical usage of خواندن = to call/appoint), completing the verb phrase "he assigned daily rations."
segments.s464-g5: سر ماه is a temporal expression "the head/start of the month" (سر = 'top/beginning', ماه = 'month'); را is the object marker attached to this phrase; لشکر آباد کرد is a causative/resultative phrase "he made the army settled/prosperous" or "he quartered/established the army," with کرد as the past auxiliary verb.
segments.s464-g6: سر نامداران is "the heads of the renowned/illustrious ones" (سر = 'head', نامداران = 'renowned/illustrious'), پر از is the adjectival construction "full of," باد literally means "wind" but here is a figurative image ("wind" = conceit/pride), and کرد is the past verb "he made/caused."
segments.s464-g7: دگر باره is a poetic adverbial phrase meaning “once again” (دگر = again + باره = instance); it modifies the whole clause. از آب is a prepositional phrase “across/from the water.” زان سو is an archaic locative adverb (زان = از آن, سو = side) meaning “to that side.” گذشت is the past tense, 3rd‑person singular of گذشتن (“he passed/crossed”), with the subject omitted and understood from context.
segments.s464-g8: بیاراست here is a past, 3rd‑person singular verb (بیا راستن) meaning “he arrayed/arranged.” لشکر is the direct object “(the) army” (no explicit possessive in the phrase; the agent is implicit). بران is a contracted locative (بر + آن) meaning “on/thereupon” and links the verb+object to the location; پهن دشت is a noun phrase “wide plain” (پهن modifies دشت). The verb preceding its object and the compact placement of the locative before the noun are typical of classical/poetic word order.
segments.s464-g49: سپاه (subject) begins the clause; انجمن شد is a light copular/resultative construction (شد ‘became’) meaning “assembled/formed an assembly.” The prepositional phrase به ایوان شاه follows the verb and gives location (‘into/at the king’s iwan’), so the basic order is Subject – Predicate – Location, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s464-g50: نهادند is the 3rd‑person plural past transitive ‘they placed/set (down).’ The remaining phrase names the thing placed: زرین (adjective) + یکی (indefinite/one) + زیرگاه (noun). The adjective precedes the numeral and noun in this poetic order; یکی functions as ‘a certain/one’ (indefinite).
segments.s464-g51: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when.’ دارا here functions as the subject (a name/title or ‘the possessor’ in the text) and is followed by the locative/relational pronoun بران ‘upon that/thereon.’ کرسی زر is the noun phrase ‘throne of gold’ (noun + gold), and نشست is the simple past ‘sat.’ The clause is a standard temporal subordinate: ‘When X sat ...’.
segments.s464-g52: برفتند is 3rd‑person plural past ‘they departed/went away.’ گردان خسروپرست is the group that moves: گردان ‘troop/retinue’ modified by the following compound adjective خسروپرست (‘Khosrow‑worshipping’/sycophantic toward the king). The verb precedes the noun phrase (verb–subject order), a frequent poetic arrangement.
segments.s464-g53: به ایرانیان is an indirect‑object prepositional phrase ‘to the Iranians’; گفت is the past verb ‘(he) said.’ کای is a vocative particle ‘O!’ and مهتران is the vocative plural ‘great ones/chiefs’ (poetic address). The sequence places the addressee after the verb, marking direct speech or an address to the assembly.
segments.s464-g54: This is a vocative/appositive list of groups being addressed: خردمند ‘the wise’, و (and), شیران literally ‘lions’ used metaphorically for brave men, و (and), جنگاوران ‘warriors.’ The nouns stand in simple coordinate structure after the initial address.
segments.s464-g55: ببینید is the imperative plural ‘look/consider’ addressed to the assembly. تا introduces the content clause ‘what the counsel of battle is’ (here functioning like ‘that/what’), followed by رای پیکار ‘the counsel/plan of battle’ and the copula چی + ست → ‘what ... is.’ The structure is Imperative + subordinate interrogative clause.
segments.s464-g56: همی is a frequentative/adverb meaning ‘continually/still’ in classical Persian; گفت is ‘he said’ (past). با درد means ‘with pain/sorrow’ (درد here emotional sorrow), و چَندی ‘and for a while’ (چندی = temporarily), and گریست is the simple past ‘he wept.’ The line combines reported speech with an emotive reaction.
segments.s464-g25: بباشید is the polite/ plural imperative 'be' (3rd person plural/formal address). It is followed by the adjective ایمن 'safe' and the prepositional phrase به ایوان خویش 'in/at your own hall'; خویش is formed from خوی + enclitic ش (his/your/their) and functions as the possessive 'own'. Word order here is imperative → adjective → location phrase.
segments.s464-g26: این عبارت is a verbless clause where سپرده is a past participle/adjectival form 'entrusted' and takes یزدان as the indirect object introduced by به 'to/unto'. The noun phrase تن و جان خویش 'your body and soul' is the thing entrusted; خوی + ش again gives the possessive. The construction implies '(having) entrusted (your) body and soul to God.'
segments.s464-g27: به جان و تن is an adverbial/prepositional phrase ('in/with life and body' — i.e. 'in person and in body'); از رومیان 'from the Romans' marks the source/agent of danger; رسته‌اید is a perfect/passive meaning 'you have been saved/spared' (2nd person plural). The overall sense: 'you have been delivered from the Romans in life and body.'
segments.s464-g28: اگر چه functions together as a concessive 'although' (literally 'if that'), introducing the contrasting clause. به خون دستها شسته‌اید is literal 'you have washed your hands in blood' — شسته‌اید is perfect (2pl) of شستن 'to wash', and به خون marks the means/by which the hands were washed; the phrase is idiomatic for having blood on one’s hands.
segments.s464-g29: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. ایرانیان is the subject 'the Iranians', ایمنی is the object 'safety/security', and یافتند is the simple past 'they obtained/found' (3pl). Word order is subject → object → verb, giving 'when the Iranians obtained safety.'
segments.s464-g30: همه رخ 'all (their) faces' is a collective noun phrase; سوی رومیان 'toward the Romans' is the directional phrase; تافتند is the past tense (3pl) of تابیدن used here in the sense 'turned (their faces) toward' (i.e. they faced the Romans). The verb agrees with the plural subject implied by 'همه'.
segments.s464-g31: سکندر is the subject 'Alexander'. بیامد is the simple past 'came'. به دشت نبرد is a locative phrase 'to the plain/field of battle' (به + noun phrase). The sequence is subject → verb → location.
segments.s464-g32: همه خواسته here is a participial/nominal phrase meaning roughly 'having summoned everyone' or 'after calling all' (خواسته functions as a past participle). سربسر is an adverb 'entirely/ everywhere', گرد + کرد is a verbal compound where گرد (around/about) combines with کردن 'to make/do' to yield 'he gathered/caused to assemble'. The direct object (the troops/men) is omitted but understood from context.
segments.s464-g9: چو + past verb introduces a temporal subordinate clause: 'چو بشنید' = 'when he heard'. The following clause 'لشکر براند' is a reported finite clause (subject 'لشکر' + verb 'براند') meaning 'the army was advancing/driving on'; word order is subject–verb as is usual in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s464-g10: 'پذیره شد' is a nominal/predicative phrase (پذیره 'reception/acceptance') plus شد as the past copula/auxiliary → 'was accepted' / 'he accepted'. The conjunction 'و' links the two actions; 'سازش آنجا بماند' = 'the settlement/truce remained there' with آنجا 'there' and بماند (past narrative form of ماندن) as the main verb meaning 'remained'.
segments.s464-g11: سپه را uses را to topicalize/emphasize 'the army/host'. چو introduces the temporal clause 'when...'; 'روی اندرآمد به روی' is a poetic repetition meaning 'face came to face' = 'came face to face' (اندرآمد here = 'appeared/entered upon'), so the whole segment reads 'when the army came face to face'.
segments.s464-g12: Subject 'زمان و زمین' (time and earth) is followed by the verb گشت 'became/turned' and the predicative adjective پرخاشجوی 'aggressive/hostile'. This is a simple past-result clause: 'time and earth became aggressive.'
segments.s464-g13: Literal order: 'سه روز اندران رزمشان شد درنگ' — 'سه روز' (three days) + locative 'اندران' (in/among them) sets the time/place; 'رزمشان' = 'their fighting'; 'شد درنگ' = 'a pause happened' (درنگ 'pause' as noun, شد as past copula/auxiliary). So: 'For three days a pause befell their fighting.'
segments.s464-g14: چنان گشت = 'it became so'; کز introduces the result/cause clause 'that from/because of ...' 'کشته شد' is the passive/past ('many were slain'), and 'جای تنگ' is a predicate meaning 'the place became narrow/cramped'. Together: 'It became so that, because many were slain, the place grew cramped.'
segments.s464-g15: فراوان (preposed adverb) + ز ایرانیان indicates source/agent in poetic usage: 'from the Iranians in abundance' → 'many Iranians'. 'کشته شد' is the passive past 'were killed/slain.'
segments.s464-g16: The construction 'جهانگیر را روز برگشته شد' uses را to topicalize or mark the person for whom the following event pertains: 'for Jahangir (جهانگیر)، the day of return arrived.' برگشته شد is a past/perfect phrase 'the return occurred' with روز as the temporal subject: 'the day to return arrived.'
segments.s464-g57: چنین گفت is a short, literary clause meaning “thus he said” (چنین = thus; گفت is the past of گفتن). کامروز مردن به نام uses the infinitive مردن as a verbal noun “to die”; کامروز (today/this very day) is an adverbial placed before it; به نام is a prepositional phrase literally “with a name,” i.e. “with a good name / honor / reputation.” The whole segment states the proposition that dying today with a good name is the preferred option.
segments.s464-g58: به از is a classical comparative construction meaning “better than.” The bracketed phrase زنده دشمن بدو شادکام is the thing being compared: زنده (alive) qualifies دشمن (enemy), بدو is the dative/enclitic pronoun “to him / at him,” and شادکام is a predicate/adjective “glad/joyful.” Word order is classical: the comparative particle precedes the contrasted clause, and the enclitic pronoun attaches to show whose rejoicing it is.
segments.s464-g59: نیاکان و شاهان ما is the noun phrase “our ancestors and kings.” تا here has a temporal/conditional sense “while / as long as,” and بدند is an archaic past form of بودن meaning “they were” (classical style). Together the clause sets a time-frame (“while our ancestors and kings were [alive]”) for what follows.
segments.s464-g60: به هر سال = “each year.” باژی is a noun “a cry / shout” (a ritual or ceremonial exclamation). همی is the older continuous/habitual marker (they used to …), and بستدند is the past plural verb; in narrative/poetic use it carries the sense “they would perform / they would conclude / they would mark” (here: mark the year with a cry).
segments.s464-g61: به هر کار = “in every affair / in every matter.” ما را contains the object marker را, marking “us” as the affected party. زبون بود is a predicative construction from زبون (abject/humbled) + بود (past of بودن) meaning “was humbled / were lowly.” روم at the end names the agent (Byzantium/Rome) in classical word order, so the clause reads “in every affair we were humbled by Rome.”
segments.s464-g62: کنون = now. بخت آزادگان is a genitival phrase “the fortune/luck of the free (nobles).” گشت is the light verb “became/turned,” and شوم is a jussive/subjunctive first‑person form meaning “may I become” or more idiomatically here “may it turn (to me).” The clause expresses a wish: “now may the fortune of the free turn (to me).”
segments.s464-g63: همه پادشاهی = “the whole kingdom / all kingship.” In classical Persian the object phrase appears before the subject; سکندر is the subject (Alexander) and گرفت is the past verb “he seized / took.” The segment reports a simple past transitive action: Alexander took the entire realm.
segments.s464-g64: جهاندار شد is a nominal + شد “became” construction: جهاندار (world‑holder / sovereign) + شد (became) = “he became ruler of the world.” تخت و افسر گرفت coordinates two objects (تخت = throne; افسر = helmet/crown) joined by و, with گرفت repeating the past transitive verb “he seized / took.” The sequence shows result (he became ruler) followed by the concrete acts (he seized throne and helmet).
segments.s464-g17: پر از درد is a preposed adjectival phrase: پر (here 'full') + از ('of') + درد ('pain'), literally 'full of pain.' برگشت is the simple past 3rd‑person verb '(he) returned.' ز آمدگاه is the poetic form of از آمدگاه ('from the battlefield'), with ز used instead of از; the clause order is adjective phrase → verb → prepositional phrase, typical of Ferdowsi's elevated style.
segments.s464-g18: چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when/for.' ندادش is the past‑tense negative verb (نداد) with the enclitic object pronoun -ش = 'him' attached (i.e. 'did not give him'). خداوند ماه follows the verb for poetic inversion and reads as a nominal phrase 'the lord/sovereign of the moon' (خداوند = 'lord/sovereign', ماه = 'moon').
segments.s464-g19: سکندر بیامد is straightforward past tense 'Alexander came.' پس is a temporal/sequence adverb 'then.' او is the pronominal subject 'he.' چو here means 'like/as,' introducing a simile, and گرد is used metaphorically as 'dust/swirl' (a frequent poetic image), so چو گرد = 'like a swirl/dust.' The simile modifies the following action.
segments.s464-g20: بسی functions as an adverb 'much/very' (classical usage). از introduces the object of یاد کرد ('remembered of/about'). جهان‌آفرین is a compound noun 'world‑creator / the Creator.' یاد کرد is a common compound of noun + simple verb meaning 'he remembered' or 'he made remembrance of' (کرد is the light verb). Word order is adverb → prepositional object → verb.
segments.s464-g21: خروشی is an abstract noun 'a cry/clamor.' برآمد is the past‑tense verb 'arose/issued forth.' ز (poetic form of از) marks origin 'from,' and پیش سپاه means 'in front of/in the presence of the army.' The basic structure is Subject (noun) → Verb → source prepositional phrase.
segments.s464-g22: که‌ای here introduces a vocative/exclamatory address—'O (you) ...'—a typical archaic/poetic form. زیردستان is the addressee 'subordinates/subjects.' گم‌کرده is a past‑participle meaning 'having lost' (i.e. 'lost' or 'missing'), and راه is 'the way/path.' Together زیردستانِ گم‌کرده راه = 'subordinates who have lost the way'; the participle follows or closely precedes the noun in classical order.
segments.s464-g23: شما را uses را as a topical/emphatic marker placing 'you' as the affected party. ز من is the prepositional phrase 'from me.' بیم و آزار is a coordinated noun phrase 'fear and harm.' نیست negates existence: 'there is no fear or harm from me towards you' (i.e. 'you needn't fear me nor be harmed by me').
segments.s464-g24: سپاه مرا literally 'my army' (مرا functions here as a possessive/agent relation in classical usage). با شما کار نیست is an idiomatic negation: 'has no business/affair with you' — کار ('affair') plus نیست ('is not') yields 'there is no affair/concern.' The clause asserts that the speaker's host will not intervene against the addressees.

sentences.s464-s17: He bestowed upon his army what he wished; it was arrayed with military strength.
sentences.s464-s18: They remained in that land and region for four months; when the king and the army were at rest...
sentences.s464-s19: The world-ruler, Dara, reached Jahrom, where he there gave the key to the treasures.
sentences.s464-s20: All the chiefs came forward; they arrived full of pain, burning heat and anguish.
sentences.s464-s21: The son, howling, when he did not see his father; and the father likewise when he did not see the son.
sentences.s464-s22: The whole city of Iran was full of lamentation; within the inner eyes the water was like dew.
sentences.s464-s23: From Jahrom he came to the city of Istakhr, which was a pride to the free (nobles).
sentences.s464-s24: A messenger went in every direction, to every notable and to every side.
sentences.s464-s33: That state did not last either; now all of Pars will become like a sea of blood.
sentences.s464-s34: Women and children and men become captives; on this land neither the young nor the old remains.
sentences.s464-s35: If you become my supporters in this (matter), I will avert this suffering and pain and harm.
sentences.s464-s36: This troupe became the prey of the great ones; all have become weary of the city of Iran.
sentences.s464-s45: Let us bind our hems one into another — if we find soil, if (we find) a home and land.
sentences.s464-s46: He gave weapons and dirhams to his army — (and) to those famed men of his country.
sentences.s464-s37: Now we are the quarry, and they (are) the leopards.
sentences.s464-s38: If you can turn their entire rear onto (their) backs, bring their towns and homeland into your fist.
sentences.s464-s39: If anyone shows weakness in this battle, let him strive to preserve his life.
sentences.s464-s40: From now on put no hope in the world; (for) Rome became Zahhāk and we (became) Jamshīd.
sentences.s464-s41: He kept saying, weeping, his heart full of pain — his two cheeks pale and his two lips lapis-blue.
sentences.s464-s42: The wise elders rose up; they all fashioned his answer.
sentences.s464-s43: A cry arose from Iran, lamenting: 'We will not allow the world to be without a king.'
sentences.s464-s44: Let us all turn our faces altogether to war; let us make the world narrow for the ill‑minded.
sentences.s464-s1: When Dāra left Alexander's presence, he dispatched horsemen in every direction — to Taft.
sentences.s464-s2: He summoned the chiefs and guests from Iran; he gave dirhams and assigned daily rations to their mouths (i.e. provided sustenance).
sentences.s464-s3: At the start of the month he stationed the army, and he filled the heads of the famed with wind (i.e. made them proud).
sentences.s464-s4: Once more he crossed the water to that side and arrayed his army there on the wide plain.
sentences.s464-s25: The host assembled in the king's iwan; they set down a single golden low-seat.
sentences.s464-s26: When Dāra sat upon that golden throne, the Khosrow‑worshipping troop moved off.
sentences.s464-s27: He said to the Iranians, “O chiefs,” — (addressing) the wise, the lions (brave men) and the warriors.
sentences.s464-s28: “Consider (decide) what the counsel for battle is,” he kept saying in sorrow, and for a while he wept.
sentences.s464-s13: Be safe in your own hall; your body and soul entrusted to God.
sentences.s464-s14: You are safe in life and body from the Romans, although you have washed your hands in blood.
sentences.s464-s15: When the Iranians found safety, everyone turned their faces toward the Romans.
sentences.s464-s16: Alexander came to the field of battle; he called everyone together and gathered them all.
sentences.s464-s5: When Alexander heard that the army was advancing, he accepted, and the settlement remained there.
sentences.s464-s6: When the host came face to face, time and earth turned aggressive.
sentences.s464-s7: For three days there was a pause in their fighting; it became such that, because of the slain, the place grew cramped.
sentences.s464-s8: Many Iranians were slain; the day came for Jahangir to return.
sentences.s464-s29: Thus he said: better to die today with a good name than to be alive and give pleasure to the enemy.
sentences.s464-s30: Our ancestors and kings, as long as they existed, used to mark each year with a shout.
sentences.s464-s31: In every affair we were humbled by Rome; now may the fortune of the free turn to me.
sentences.s464-s32: Alexander seized the whole kingdom; he became a world‑ruler and took the throne and the helmet.
sentences.s464-s9: He returned from the battlefield full of pain, for the lord of the moon gave him no aid.
sentences.s464-s10: Alexander came; then he, like a swirl of dust, greatly remembered the Creator.
sentences.s464-s11: A clamor rose before the army: 'O you subordinates who have lost the way!'
sentences.s464-s12: You need not fear or expect harm from me; my army has nothing to do with you.
